parentQuestion: A marathon runner develops severe knee pain. MRI shows a torn meniscus (fibrocartilage). The orthopedic surgeon explains this will heal very slowly and may require surgical repair. What property of this tissue makes healing so difficult?

scaffolds.q: What is required for tissue healing?
options: Delivery of nutrients, oxygen, and repair cells to the injury
scaffolds.explain: Healing requires DELIVERY OF NUTRIENTS, OXYGEN, AND REPAIR CELLS to the injury site. Without these supplies, tissues can't repair themselves effectively.

wrongExplains.0: Time alone isn't enough without healing supplies.
wrongExplains.2: Rest helps but supplies must still reach the injury.
wrongExplains.3: Antibiotics fight infection but don't repair tissue.

scaffolds.q: How do healing supplies usually reach injured tissue?
options: Through blood vessels delivering nutrients and cells
scaffolds.explain: BLOOD VESSELS deliver healing supplies directly to injured tissues. Good blood supply = fast healing. Blood brings immune cells, growth factors, and nutrients.

wrongExplains.0: Diffusion through skin is too slow and limited.
wrongExplains.2: Neighboring organs don't supply healing factors.
wrongExplains.3: Nerves transmit signals, not healing supplies.

scaffolds.explain: VASCULAR means HAVING BLOOD VESSELS. 'A-vascular' means WITHOUT blood vessels. The 'a-' prefix means 'not' or 'without.'
Cartilage is AVASCULAR — it has NO BLOOD VESSELS. Nutrients reach cartilage cells only by slow diffusion from surrounding tissues.

wrongExplains.2: No part of cartilage is vascular.

scaffolds.q: How do nutrients reach cartilage cells?
options: By slow diffusion through the cartilage matrix
scaffolds.explain: Nutrients reach cartilage by SLOW DIFFUSION through the matrix. This is much slower than blood delivery — days instead of minutes.

scaffolds.q: What is the consequence of being avascular for healing?
options: Very slow healing or no healing at all
scaffolds.explain: Avascular tissues have VERY SLOW HEALING or NO HEALING at all. Without blood vessel delivery, repair cells and supplies can't reach the injury efficiently.

wrongExplains.0: Avascular tissues heal slower, not faster.
wrongExplains.3: Time of day doesn't affect healing rate.

scaffolds.q: Why might a torn meniscus require surgery?
options: Because it won't heal well on its own due to poor blood supply
scaffolds.explain: Surgery may be needed because the meniscus WON'T HEAL WELL on its own due to POOR BLOOD SUPPLY. Surgeons may remove or repair the damaged portion.

wrongExplains.0: Surgery can't add blood vessels to cartilage.
wrongExplains.2: Infection prevention isn't the main reason.

scaffolds.q: What tissue type is the meniscus made of?
scaffolds.explain: The knee meniscus is made of FIBROCARTILAGE. It provides shock absorption between the femur and tibia during weight-bearing activities.

wrongExplains.0: Hyaline covers joint surfaces; fibrocartilage forms menisci.
wrongExplains.2: Elastic cartilage is in ears and epiglottis.

scaffolds.q: What property of the meniscus makes healing so difficult?
scaffolds.explain: The meniscus is AVASCULAR (LACKS BLOOD SUPPLY). Without blood vessels, healing supplies must diffuse slowly from the edges, making repair very slow or impossible.

wrongExplains.2: Cartilage has few cells, not too many.
